instructorNote: Review each debate carefully. Pay attention to how teams use evidence, address counterarguments, and structure their reasoning. You will need to select who you believe won each debate and explain why on the exam.

debates.description: Should California prioritize Housing First with treatment programs, or a step-by-step approach with housing centers then rehabilitation?

teamA.name: Group 1
teamA.position: Housing First + Mental Health/Substance Abuse Treatment + Financial Incentives
keyStats: 82% of homeless individuals have mental health conditions
73% effectiveness of financial incentives for treatment
44% of chronically homeless Americans are in California

teamB.name: Group 2
teamB.position: Housing First with step-by-step approach: housing centers first, then expand to rehab facilities
keyStats: 9 out of 10 people remain housed after 1 year
Housing first increases engagement in job training and healthcare

segments.label: Opening Statement
prompts: What is Group 1's core policy proposal? Identify the three components.
How does Group 1 define 'chronic homelessness' and why does this definition matter for their argument?

transcript.text: Homelessness is a big problem in California.
California has the highest total homeless population in the nation, which is driven by unaffordable housing, mental illness, substance abuse, and more.
There are currently more than 187,000 homeless people in California, both sheltered and unsheltered.
These people do not have housing stability, which is the ability to maintain which is the ability to access and maintain safe, consistent, and affordable housing.
Moreover, almost a fourth of all homeless Americans in California, including 44% of Americans that are chronically homeless.

Chronic homelessness is when someone with a disabling condition has been homeless continuously for a year or more or has had four episodes of homelessness in the past three years, which total over a year.

Currently, California's approach to solving homelessness is housing first, which helps homeless people access housing without requiring sobriety or treatment participation as a precondition while offering voluntary supportive services in the long-term supporting house of C housing category.
This program makes housing affordable with long-term rental assistance with low no limit on how young people can stay.
We believe that the best policy to address the homelessness crisis in California is long-term supportive housing combined with mental health and substance use resources provided.
We are going to supplement California's current approach of housing first with financial incentives for treatment because it improves housing stability and addresses chronic homelessness.
Thank you very much.

outline.title: Scale of Homelessness in California
bullets: Driven by unaffordable housing, mental illness, substance abuse

outline.title: Defining Key Terms
bullets: Housing stability: access safe, consistent, affordable housing
Chronic homelessness: disabling condition + homeless 1+ years
44% of chronically homeless Americans in California

outline.title: Our Policy Proposal
bullets: Supplement Housing First with financial incentives
Combined with mental health and substance use resources
Improves housing stability and addresses chronic homelessness

segments.label: Opening Statement
prompts: What is Group 2's main argument for why housing must come before other solutions?
What evidence does Group 2 cite to support the effectiveness of Housing First?

transcript.text: Every night in Santa Clara County, over 10,000 people face the reality of homelessness.
But the real issue isn't just the lack of shelter.
It's the lack of stability.
Current approaches often try to fix addiction or employment before housing.
But without a stable place to live, those solutions rarely succeed.

Our policy proposes that Santa Clara County prioritize funding housing centers for individuals experiencing chronic homelessness first and then expand funding to rehab facilities for those with illnesses.
This housing first approach focuses on creating stability before addressing other challenges.
The evidence is clear.
According to the US Department of Housing and Urban Development, about nine out of 10 people remain housed one year after receiving housing housing first assistance.
In addition, providing immediate housing increases engagement in job training, health care, and rehabilitation programs, leading to long-term success rather than temporary fixes.
By by prioritizing stable housing first and then addressing underlying issues, this policy offers a more effective, humane, and economically sustainable solution to homelessness in our community.

outline.title: The Crisis in Santa Clara County
bullets: Real issue is lack of stability, not just shelter

outline.title: Current Approaches Fail
bullets: Trying to fix addiction/employment before housing fails
Without stable housing, other solutions rarely succeed

outline.title: Our Policy: Housing Centers First
bullets: Prioritize funding housing centers for chronically homeless

outline.title: Evidence: Housing First Works
bullets: Increases engagement in job training, healthcare, rehab
More effective, humane, and economically sustainable

segments.label: Main Arguments
prompts: List the economic evidence Group 1 uses. How do they connect cost savings to their policy?
How does Group 1 argue that financial incentives improve treatment outcomes? What specific study do they cite?
What is the relationship between mental health treatment and housing stability in Group 1's argument?

transcript.text: So connecting chronic homelessness with permanent housing is an effective solution.
The reason behind the success of our policy is contingent on California's current implementation of the housing first program and the impacts it has on addressing the homelessness crisis.
The program finds that those using housing first are able to move to permanent housing faster and 90% of housing first tenants were able to retain housing stability.

Additionally, in a larger scope to demonstrate the achievable nature of the plan, a pathways to housing program in 2004 highlighted how housing first produces a housing retention of 80% therefore causing a decrease in chronic homelessness.
Similar to the alternative policy, this policy concurs the housing first with long-term supportive housing is a necessary for addressing chronic homelessness and increasing housing stability.
Housing first works because stabil stable housing is the foundation that makes treatment and recovery more effective rather than requiring people to solve those issues before having a place to live.
Rather than treatment first, housing first and more specifically, our policy combats homelessness by providing stable housing and access to the resources they need.
Both based on evidence from California's approach and strategies across the country, housing first is demonstrated to help homeless homeless individuals.
Housing first is one of the most effective ways to offer support without sobriety or treatment as a prere prerequisite.
Services are voluntary and preser prefers self-guidance towards sobriety.

Therefore, by providing treatment to individuals who may need it in a stable source of housing, the plan actualized what a housing and human development study finds when comparing housing first to treatment first.
The policy could save $6,37 annually per homeless adult with a chronic medical condition and 9,89 per year for chronic chronically homeless individuals.
The predicted economic savings could be 5.5 billion over the next 10 years by providing stable housing and resources.
Housing first is a better more cost effective strategy.
In addition to providing housing, it is imperative that we provide treatment for the homeless, which is effect which is effective in addressing the struggles they face in staying home.
According to Proposition One, 82% of the homeless population in California experience mental health conditions and one in 10 Californians who meet the criteria for substance abuse disorders.
Homelessness cannot be effectively treated without treating the underlying problems that keep people from having stable.

The high rates of mental health conditions and substance abuse disorders present in homeless present in homeless populations makes it difficult for them to support themselves without receiving treatment and increases the likelihood of them being incarcerated and remaining chronically homeless.
With 82% of these individuals experiencing mental health challenges as previously stated, it is imperative that any policy addresses individuals who are at the highest risk of becoming homeless.
Providing treatment is actually key to reducing this chronic homelessness and it cannot be ignored at any rate.

The current rate for individuals returning to homeless populations that were only placed in temporary housing in California is around 23% according to the USC homelessness policy and research institute.

While this is undeniably a low number compared to other criminal populations, such as 39.1% for recidivism in general, according to the California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation, there is still a long way to go, especially when the size of the homeless population in California is taken into account.
Providing more than just temporary subsidized housing will go a long way in lowering these rates even further.
And providing treatment will enable these homeless populations to live with stability.
In order to help homeless people go to and stay in treatment, we are going to have financial incentives.
Financial incentives such as subsidies for renting and covering groceries have statistically been linked with pursuing treatment.

A 2021 National Library of Medicine study concludes that financial incentives are 73% effective in getting people to treatment and improving health outcomes such as therapy adherence and substance use abstinence.
And the treatment specifically noted that it's majorly effective among people who are experiencing homelessness and or mental illness.
Mental illness and substance use are one of the biggest causes of homelessness.
Um a UCSF benny of homelessness and housing initiative study finds that four out of five unhoused people in California who were surveyed reported experiencing a serious mental health condition.
In order to help more people achieve housing stability and increased access to treatment, it is important to target these barriers through supportive treatment.
Financial incentives are the best way to ensure that as many people as possible go to treatment, get better, and are able to support themselves.
The impact of financial incentives is pervasive and necessary for addressing homelessness.
The same study finds that at least one in five unhoused individuals who struggle with mental health problems are unable to receive or access treatment.
By incentivizing and providing treatment to people living in long-term supportive housing, we are able to not only end the cycle of chronic homelessness, but provide necessary support.
Thank you.

segments.label: Main Arguments
prompts: How does Group 2 structure their argument around 'stability as foundation'?
What real-world program data does Group 2 present? How does it support their step-by-step approach?
How does Group 2 respond to the argument that 'housing alone is not enough'?

transcript.text: The most effective way to address chronic homelessness in Santa Clara County is to prioritize a housing first approach because it creates stability, improves long-term outcomes, and ultimately reduces the overall public cost.
First, stable housing is the foundation for solving every other issue addressed with homelessness.
with a consistent place to live.
It is without a consistent place to live, it is nearly impossible for individuals to maintain employment, access health care, or participate in rehabilitation programs.

Our policy ensures that individuals experiencing chronic homelessness are given immediate access to housing centers that also provide supportive services such as job training, education, and healthcare navigation.
This creates a stable environment where individuals can begin to rebuild their lives rather than constantly focusing on day-to-day survival.
Second, the evidence strongly supports the effectiveness of this approach.
According to the US Department of Housing and Urban Development, approximately 90% of individuals remain housed after one year after receiving housing first assistance.
This demonstrates that when people are given stable housing without preconditions, they are significantly more likely to maintain their stability long term.

Additionally, research shows that time spent in housing first programs is associated with improved outcomes over multiple years, including increased participation in employment and treatment programs.
This directly refutes the argument that housing alone does not address underlying issues because in reality, it enables people to actually engage with these solutions.
Third, this policy benefits not only individuals experiencing homelessness, but also the broader community and the economy.
By reducing chronic homelessness, the strain on emergency services, shelters, and public healthare systems decreases.
While opponents could argue that housing first is too expensive, this perspective ignores the long-term cost savings.

Providing stable housing with supportive services is more coste effective than just repeatedly funding emergency interventions such as hospital visits, law enforcement involvement, and temporary shelters.
In other words, this policy is not just compassionate, but it's more financially responsible for the economy.
Finan or finally, our policy builds on this success by expanding into rehabilitation facilities for individuals with mental illness or substance use disorders after housing stability is achieved.
This step-by-step approach ensures that individuals are not overwhelmed and are more likely to successfully complete treatment.
By addressing both immediate needs and long-term challenges, this policy creates a sustainable pathway out of homelessness.
In conclusion, prioritizing housing centers first addresses the root cause of instability in is supported by strong evidence and leads to better outcomes for both individuals and the community.
This makes it the most effective and realistic solution to homelessness crisis in Santa Clair County.
Um and our evidence to back up um Chloe's statements is that first one of the strongest pieces of evidence is that nine out of 10 people get housed after one year in the housing first program.
This means that 90% of people who were given stable housing were able to keep it longterm.
That's important because it shows this policy doesn't just give temporary help, it actually works in keeping people off the streets.
Next, housing first doesn't just provide shelter, it also helps people improve overall well-being.
Study shows that longer that the longer someone stays in a housing first program, the more their alcohol use improves over a two-year period.
This means people are not only staying housed, but they are also making healthier life choices.
Instead of punishing people for struggling, this approach gives them stability first, which makes recovery more possible.

Finally, this policy has already helped thousands of people, over 5,545 people who have been contracted and enrolled in programs like PATH and provided employment support, housing assistance, and rapid rehousing services.
This shows that housing first is not just an idea is being used making a real impact on people's lives.

Overall, this evidence proves that housing first is effective because it keeps people housed, improves their health over time, and helps large numbers of people get back on their feet instead of cycling people through shelters or jails.
Gives them real chance at stability and recovery.

bullets: Without housing, impossible to maintain employment or healthcare

So when we look at the evidence, we need to talk about why preventing mental health and substance use support simultaneously is better rather than just providing housing first and waiting for that stability that's never going to come.

So 82% of homeless people experience mental health conditions and one out of 10 Californians meet the criteria for substance use disorders and this is based on Prop One that's been implemented in California.
Looking at Marggo Kushell who is the professor of medicine and director of the UCSF Benoff homelessness and housing in initiative.

A 2025 study finds that she explained that complex behavioral needs including substance and mental health problems increases the risk of becoming homeless and mental health and exacerbates these problems.
So the thing that we need to understand is that go going into homelessness makes these problem worse.
But these problems are also the cause of it.
So we don't address it in the first place.
Housing stability can never be reached.
And this situation is immediate as the California statewide study of people experiencing homelessness from 2023 fines.

And among participants who use drugs in the study, 42% began using more after the first time they became homelessness.
And finally, 28% of individuals who were participated in this study found that they struggled with heavy alcohol and regular drug use.
They could not access desired treatment homeless.
So even if they manage to provide housing and they don't act until later on, we can never guarantee that they're able to fully prosper and like engage in society after receiving this housing.
There's clearly a need for why the policy should focus on providing substance use and behavioral health support to homeless individuals.
Homelessness continues the cycle of substance abuse and mental health challenges.
exacerbating them and increasing the rates of chronic homelessness.
And moreover, we need to talk about why this treatment worked and why it should be provided simultaneously.
An NIH study in 2025 focused on the connection between homelessness and trorbid chronic medical conditions, serious mental illness and substance use disorders.
It found that by providing homeless individuals with health support, it decreased the use of emergency department resources and the severity of behavioral health syn.
This study is important for understanding our argument of how providing support and resources at the same time is necessary for increasing the success rate of this policy.

And finally, we talk about learning how we need to incentivize treatment because even if the alternative group and even if their policy argues that they provide that treatment, they have no way to incentivize that which means that treatment will never be like accessed and that means like loss of hundreds of thousands of taxpayer dollars.
So we are able to incentivize this treatment.

Going off what our main argument talked about, a 2021 National Library of Medicine study concludes that financial incentives are 73% effective in getting people to treatment and improving health outcomes such as therapy adherence and substance use abstinence.
It's also majorly effective specifically amongst people experiencing homelessness and mental illness by pursuing a housing first policy that combines both housing with support.
The policy does not make receiving housing on accessing treatment, but it does provide the resources to those who need it.

Therefore, to boost involvement in these support options, we aim to provide financial incentives such as reducing rental subsidies and providing additional financial support to ensuring stable employment.
Therefore, unlike our alternative policy, we're able to implicate our argument and talk how we're able to provide incentives for their treatment, how they're able to access it.
And finally, we're able to show how our treatment can be implemented and is more feasible.
Our policy is something that's not rooted in theory but actually been implemented in Santa CL County.

The study offers which a study that's been conducted in Santa Clara finds that it provides subsidized housing with intensive case management with support teams led by behavioral health professional and one staff member for every 15 clients.
It's very similar to the strategy that we're talking about today.
This strategy specifically resulted in 86% of participants remaining in housing for at least several years and 93% of all individuals in the study remaining house throughout its duration.
We're also able to prove that we've been able to implement in Santa CL County and has direct effects on increasing housing stability and reducing chronic homelessness.
Margaret Koshel again as the director of UCSF Benoff homelessness and housing initiatives echoed this idea.
She find the bottom line is that really high-risisk folks must be housed and key word is must.
We must be able to house them and we must be able to provide treatment in order to address the homelessness crisis.
And finally, regarding cost, they mention how they're able to provide economic benefits but don't exactly state how.
So we're able to show how we're able to directly pay put people into housing, how we're able to reduce the cost and add to the economy.

In a comprehensive analysis of cost from treatment first and housing first programs, the HUD adds that housing first would take 6,37 annually per homeless adult with a chronic medical condition at 9,89 per year for chronically homeless individuals.
The predicted economic savings will be 5.5 billion added to the economy, which means we've been able to scale and expand this program.

And this is why we were able to implicate how we're able to provide in we were able to provide incentives to make this program a reality and how we can end the cycle of homelessness by pro ending chronic homelessness and promoting housing stability in Santa Clara and the wider California.
Thank you.

segments.label: Closing Statement
prompts: What new evidence does Group 1 introduce in their closing? (Hint: meta-analysis)
How does Group 1 distinguish their policy from Group 2's in their final summary?

transcript.text: We've said like we've said homelessness in California is an urgent crisis affecting over 187,000 people driven by mental illness, substance abuse, and most of all, high housing costs.
Nearly a quarter of the nation's homeless reside in California.
The statistics discussed in support of our policy demonstrate how there is a growing need among the homeless population to access stable housing and treatment options.

With 25% of homeless individuals facing severe mental illness and 24% experiences experiencing substance use disorders is imperative that we uh or that better policy housing first today provide or housing first provides for the needs of the most vulnerable.

Furthermore, with the opposition arguing against the prioritization of mental health and substance uh assistance, uh we argue that prioritizing these individuals will not generate a more sustainable impact by ensuring housing stability but will also lower rates or returning homeless or returning to homelessness.
Regarding the welfare of homelessness, a metaanalysis of 26 studies found by or found that housing first has reduced homelessness by 88%.
It increased housing stability by 41%.
Housing stability rates or housing stability decreased the rate of uh returning to homelessness, therefore bettering the welfare of society.
By improving support services, our policy empowers and guides individuals to access resources that support their mental health and allow them to more fully engage in society.
After being able to live in a h in stable housing, program participants will be able to engage in society by having the means to pursue employment and build up financial resources.

Therefore, our program is sustained by the incent by incentivizing treatment with uh financial means because more involvement in in treatment support in treatment support correlates to a higher rate of disability.

A 2023 NIH study echoes the concept that as it finds concept as it finds a direct connection between stable housing and access and participating in economic activities like employment by promoting employment and economic activities among individuals throughout California.
Our program creates activities among individuals or create sorry by promoting economic or sorry by promoting employment and economic activities among individuals throughout California.
Our program creates barriers to the cycle of homelessness and contributes to offsetting the cost of the policy.

In conclusion, by expanding access to affordable housing through combining long-term supportive housing, comprehensive support, and lasting solutions, our policy increases the rate of housing stability and chronic homelessness.

To clarify, we are still providing housing and treatment at the same time, while the alternative policy is waiting for individuals to achieve housing stability before accessing treatment, which is not how ending chronically or is not how ending chronic homelessness works.
Evidence shows that the treatment must be combined with incentives and housing to actualize benefits to society.

Policy empowers individuals to access resources

segments.label: Closing Statement
prompts: What cost-effectiveness evidence does Group 2 present in their closing?
How does Group 2 frame the debate as 'sequential vs. simultaneous' in their conclusion?

transcript.text: Um, in conclusion, the most effective way to address chronic homelessness in Santa Clar County is to prioritize a housing first approach because it creates stability, improves long-term outcomes, and reduces overall public cost.
As our group has demonstrated throughout this presentation, the core issue behind homelessness is not just the lack of shelter.
It is the lack of stability.
Without a consistent place to live, individuals cannot reliably maintain employment, access health care, or fully engage in rehabilitation programs.
Housing must come first in order for every other solution to work.
Our evidence strongly supports this claim.
According to the US Department of Housing and Urban Development, approximately 90% of indivi individuals remain housed one year after receiving housing first assistance.
This shows that when people are given stable housing without preconditions, they are significantly more likely to maintain that stability over time.

In addition, we discussed how housing first programs increase participate participation in job training, education, healthcare, and treatment programs directly addressing the argument that housing alone is not enough.
In reality, housing is what makes these other solutions possible.
Further research from the National Alliance to End Homelessness shows that supportive housing can reduce emergency room visits and hospitalizations by up to 40 to 60%.
Demonstrating that without stable housing, individuals are forced into costly short-term crisis systems rather than long-term recovery.

Even more importantly, studies comparing out comparing approaches show that housing first models consistently outperform treatment first or simultaneous approaches with significantly higher long-term housing retention rates, often nearly double those of traditional models.
This proves that trying to solve housing and rehabilitation at the same time is less effective because it ignores the reality that stability must come before successful treatment.
We also demonstrated that this policy is not only effective but economically responsible.

A systematic review of US housing first program found that for every $1 spent, communities receive about $1.80 in economic benefits due to reduced use of emergency healthcare, shelters, and law enforcement.
This connects directly to our earlier point that current systems relying on temporary shelters but and emergency responses are actually more expensive in the long run.
By shifting resources towards stable housing, the county can reduce long-term costs while improving outcomes.
Ultimately, this policy offers a realistic, evidence-based, and sustainable solution to to chronic homelessness.
It addresses the root cause of instability, builds on proven outcomes, and benefits both individuals and the broader community.
By prioritizing housing first, Santa Clair County can move beyond temporary fixes, and take a meaningful step towards ending chronic homelessness for good.

outline.title: Core Argument: Stability First
bullets: Creates stability, improves outcomes, reduces cost
Core issue is lack of stability, not just shelter
Housing must come first for other solutions to work

outline.title: Evidence Recap
bullets: Housing first increases participation in treatment

outline.title: Sustainable Pathway Forward
bullets: Evidence-based and realistic for Santa Clara County

evaluationPrompts.prompt: Which team presented a more feasible policy? Consider funding, implementation, and scalability.
Which team used stronger evidence? Consider the quality, relevance, and recency of their sources.
Which team was more effective at countering the other's arguments? What made their rebuttal strong or weak?
Who won this debate overall? Write 2-3 sentences explaining your reasoning using specific examples from the debate.
